title: India Heat Wave Kills 37 as Temperatures Approach 50°C - Seoul Economic Daily
full_text:
A severe heat wave in India has killed 37 people from heatstroke as daytime highs approached 50 degrees Celsius.
According to The Associated Press and other foreign media on Friday, authorities in the southern state of Telangana said 16 people across the state recently died from the extreme heat.
Officials said they plan to provide compensation of $4,180 (about 6.3 million won) to bereaved families. They also issued warnings, citing a high likelihood that the heat will continue. In India, heat wave warnings are typically issued when temperatures exceed 40 degrees in plains regions and 30 degrees in mountainous areas.
The heat wave has been sweeping across India since last month. Earlier, 21 people died from heatstroke in Andhra Pradesh state. Last week, power demand reached an all-time high of 270 gigawatts, triggering large-scale blackouts in parts of southern India.
In parts of Uttar Pradesh, the country's most populous state, markets closed during daytime hours, and vehicles and pedestrians disappeared from outdoor areas. In Banda, in the Bundelkhand region, daytime highs reached 48.2 degrees last week, forcing schools to suspend classes.
The 25 hottest locations in the world are currently all in India, according to data. Varanasi, Azamgarh and Jaunpur — all in Uttar Pradesh — each recorded 47 degrees, sweeping the top three spots among the world's hottest cities.
In India, hot weather typically begins in March or April, with daytime highs reaching around 50 degrees in May. Temperatures gradually decline once the monsoon season arrives in June. The local meteorological agency forecast that extreme heat will continue through the 28th in Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh and Rajasthan.
